title: The 'try?' Shortcut 🩼
description: When you don't care *why* it failed, you just want an Optional.

slides.title: The Fast Method 🩼
slides.content:
# Using try?

Setting up a massive `do-catch` block every time is exhausting. Sometimes, you don't care *which* specific error was thrown... you just want to know if it Worked or if it Failed.

Swift provides a shortcut: `try?` (with a question mark!).

It completely skips the `do-catch` block, attempts the function, and safely returns an **Optional**! If the function fails, it silently returns `nil`!

slides.title: Look How Clean! ✨

quizData.question: If you don't want to write a full `do-catch` block to handle nuanced errors, and just want the function to silently return `nil` if it breaks, what keyword do you use?
quizData.explanation: The `try?` keyword converts any thrown error silently into a `nil`, allowing you to use perfectly safe Optionals instead of complex do-catch logic.

slides.title: See It in Action
slides.content: We can seamlessly combine `try?` with the `guard let` bouncer we learned in Chapter 5!
